options: Returns the page's HTML as a String for you to parse yourself
Navigates the browser to the URL and waits for the page to load
Sends an HTTP GET and never opens a browser window
Downloads the page to disk for offline testing
questions.explanation: get() drives the real browser to the URL and blocks until the page finishes loading, so the next line of the test runs against a loaded page.

questions.prompt: Which call returns the current page's title as a String?
questions.explanation: driver.getTitle() returns the title of the current page, a quick way to confirm you landed where you expected.

questions.prompt: What is the difference between driver.close() and driver.quit()?
options: close() ends the driver process; quit() only hides the window
They are identical aliases for the same action
close() shuts the current window; quit() shuts every window and ends the driver
quit() saves a screenshot first, close() does not
questions.explanation: close() closes just the active window. quit() closes all windows the driver opened and stops the driver process — the clean way to end a test.

questions.prompt: Why should every test end with driver.quit()?
options: Otherwise the test results are not saved
Otherwise browser and driver processes leak and pile up on the machine
Because Selenium refuses to start a new test until the old one quits
Because quit() is what actually runs the assertions
questions.explanation: Without quit(), each run leaves a browser and driver process alive. Over many runs these accumulate and degrade the machine, especially on CI.
